flashcards.front: What is swapping in memory management?
flashcards.back: Swapping is a technique where entire processes are moved between secondary storage and main memory. When memory is full, a process in memory is moved to swap space (disk) to make room for another process.
A memory allocation method where each process is assigned a single continuous block of physical memory. The entire process must fit in one continuous region.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between internal and external fragmentation?
flashcards.back: Internal fragmentation occurs when allocated memory is larger than requested (wasted space inside allocated blocks). External fragmentation occurs when free memory is scattered in small non-contiguous blocks, preventing allocation of larger processes.

flashcards.front: What is paging in memory management?
flashcards.back: Paging divides both physical and virtual memory into fixed-size blocks called frames and pages respectively. It eliminates external fragmentation by allowing non-contiguous allocation.
A page table is a data structure that maps virtual page numbers to physical frame numbers. It contains one entry for each virtual page and stores the frame address and control bits.
Segmentation divides a program into logical units like code, data, stack, and heap. Each segment has a name and size, providing logical view of memory rather than physical.

Virtual memory is a technique that allows execution of processes that may not be completely in memory. It provides illusion of having more memory than physically available using paging or segmentation.
Demand paging loads pages into memory only when they are referenced (on-demand). Pages are initially marked as invalid and loaded from secondary storage when a page fault occurs.
Copy-on-write is an optimization where parent and child processes initially share the same memory pages. A copy is made only when one process tries to modify a shared page.
FIFO (First-In-First-Out), LRU (Least Recently Used), and Optimal (OPT) are three common page replacement algorithms. Others include Second Chance and Clock algorithm.
Thrashing occurs when the system spends most of its time swapping pages in and out rather than executing processes. It happens when there are too many processes competing for too few frames.

flashcards.front: What is the working set model?
flashcards.back: The working set model defines the set of pages a process is actively using. The OS ensures each process has enough frames to accommodate its working set to minimize page faults and thrashing.
Belady's anomaly is the counterintuitive phenomenon where increasing the number of page frames can cause an increase in page faults, specifically observed in FIFO algorithm.
A page fault occurs when a process accesses a page that is not currently in physical memory. The OS must then load the required page from secondary storage.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of the valid-invalid bit in a page table?
flashcards.back: The valid-invalid bit indicates whether a page is currently in memory (valid) or not (invalid). If a process accesses an invalid page, a page fault is generated.
